sentences.text: Good morning.
My full name is David Darrio Gabbai.
G-A-B-B-A-I.
Yes
No, that's-- that's the full name.
I was born in Thessaloniki, or Salonika, Greece at that time, in September 2, 1922.
Today I'm 74 years old.
My parents, my father was Victor Gabbai and my mother was Rosa Beraha Gabbai.
Originally, we started from the Spanish Inquisition, 1492, from Spain, going across the continents.
And my father and mother were born in Smyrna, Turkey.
And then going back, they went to Salonika at that time.
And then became-- Salonika became Greece, you know, from Greece.
My-- my father worked all his life as a typographer for the Greek newspaper called which means new-- new truth.
And he worked there since we went back to-- to Poland.
My mother never worked.
Used to stay home, take care of the kids.
We were three brothers, and I was the middle one.
Jack or Jacob was the older one, and Samuel was the youngest one.
My-- my brother, the older one, was born in 1913.
I was born in 1922.
And Samuel was in 1932.
Was one system that was die at birth, you know, in the '30s.
We were Italian citizens living in Greece.
And we used to have a permit to work there, but were permanent residents of Greece.
And we had to go every year to just renew our permit to work.
Mostly I went to an Italian school, and-- where we-- we had to learn about four languages.
And it was a beautiful school.
But at that time was the era of the Fascist government in Italy.
And I finished in 1938, the high school of the Italian school.
And I was ready to go to an Italian university, study-- mostly I would like to be a doctor.
But unfortunately at that time in 1938, Hitler and Mussolini got together and they didn't let anymore Jewish Italians go to the Italian universities.
We live in-- mostly in a Jewish neighborhood.
The-- we lived in a quarter called 151 in Salonika.
Actually, the Jewish community bought all that section from-- actually, they were barracks of the Italian army from the First World War.
And when the-- when the First World War finished in 1919, that was left over, and the Jewish community bought it and we were living there practically for very little money, you know.
Well, the-- my grandparents on my-- on my mother's side living-- were living with us, in the same-- in the same-- actually in the same house.
Their names were-- her name was Allegra, you know, and his name was Bohor.
Their last name were Beraha.
Well, we had a lot on activities in the Italian school, actually a lot of athletic activities there.
And we-- I was also going every year-- the school took us for two, three months in the summer to Italy, free you know.
And at that time, I used to play in a band with a clarinet.
Yes, it was very much.
It was beautiful.
We used to go to-- they used to take us to Como, di Lago Como, and then to Cortina d'Ampezzo, you know, and then the last month we used to be in Rome.
We used to go about for seven days by boat from-- from Salonika to Italy.
And from there, it was a beautiful three months during the summer, because it didn't cost us anything.
We have-- you know, we're enough Jewish Italians-- a Jewish Italian colony in Salonika.
And lots of them were well-to-do people.
And I had quite a few friends.
And one of the-- the friends is-- was Daniel Bennahmias that later on we spent most of our lives together.
What was the Jewish community like in Salonika?
Was-- I wasn't involved too much as a Jew.
But the Jewish community of Salonika were-- before the war, the Jewish population in Salonika was close to 100,000 in 200,000 population.
It was 50%.
And I remember that on Saturdays especially the port of Salonika was closed because none of the Jewish people would work.
And downtown was closed every Saturday.
There-- there were a lot of synagogues and a lot of different other institutions that were helping the poor people of-- the Jewish poor people in Salonika.
What about Jewish observance in your family?
What was it like?
My-- my mother was pretty good in observing the Jewish tradition.
And I and my father were more liberals at that time.
My grandparents were, you know, pretty-- pretty good.
They-- my grandparents and my mother's family were pretty religious.
Very seldom I went to the synagogue with my family.
But my mother I know used to go every-- every Saturday and especially during the Jewish holidays.
The Jewish holidays, we all went together.
We were very-- they-- they celebrate every-- every holiday, you know.
There were so many-- so many Jewish institutions, so many things and schools and elementary schools and Jewish schools, you know.
So it was very-- pretty good Jewish life.
My home, we used to-- to have our Jewish holidays.
My mother always saw that we were there for the Jewish holidays.
My favorite was always Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.
Well, these were the high holidays what everybody used to get dressed and, you know, like the old-- old time.
Prepare the-- the food that the Sephardic used to-- to have.
And we were-- we were enough-- we used to be maybe 8, 10 people.
You'd get together.
And we had a very, very interesting dinner during the Jewish-- you know, the Passover.
Well, you know, they-- they-- the Sephardics made very, very good stuff.
We used to have the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] chicken and rice and beans, you know, that the part of it, and lots of fruits and vegetables.
Everything the-- no matter what, how poor we were, you know, always during the holidays, the table was always full of food.
We were-- with my older brother, I-- I wasn't too close, you know.
We were different about nine years, eight, nine years, so-- we were together in many occasions, but we weren't as close as we should be.
My younger brother was-- was too young at that time.
But we were-- a family, very loving family always from the start.
Parents always they-- they were very good parents.
I had very good parents.
Always they loved us and they gave us as much as they could do it.
My father, very, very close relationship.
Father and mother very, very close.
I used to go with my father-- I remember before he goes to work, we used to go and fish practically two, three times a week.
We used to walk in the morning, 5:30 in the morning for about 30 minutes to get to the sea from our place where we lived.
And you just go.
It was a big-- at that time, an Italian Jew had the biggest, the [NON-ENGLISH] they called it, [NON-ENGLISH].
They were making the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do they call it?
There were meals.
There were-- so-- and they had a long-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what they call it?
A long-- to go to sea-- they had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] You mean like a--
A platform, you know.
Oh, you mean like a pier.
Yeah.
A pier.
They had a pier going all the way there.
And we used to go to the pier and stay there until the time from 5:30, 6 o'clock until 8:30, when my father used to go to work.
We used to catch, they call it-- in Italian they call it branzino, which is striped bass.
We used-- yeah.
We used to take it.
In the evening, we used to have a full meal at home.
You know, my mother was waiting all the time to have a fresh-- fresh fish for dinner.
She has to clean the fish.
She had to do everything.
We use to belong-- at that time, we were Italians Jews.
We used to belong to an Italian youth organization at school.
This is the organization that used to take us to Italy every-- every year for free for vacation.
I think I had a bar mitzvah.
I don't remember exactly, but I had a bar mitzvah.
No.
Well, we were at least-- the house, we had two bedrooms, for-- one bedroom for us, one bedroom for our grandparents.
And on the other side were another two bedrooms for another family.
That was the Modiano family that lived with us for many, many years.
Yeah, Jewish Italians.
Well, the Italian Jews, we always going to Italian affairs, you know, at that time.
That's whey when some of the-- there were Italian Jews that were very powerful.
And they were also involved in all the Jewish affairs.
But we were-- but the Italians had a lot of affairs at that time, you know.
They had dances.
And so we used to go there, because we were part of it.
We speak a couple of languages.
We-- we spoke Greek, Italian, and Ladino.
We speak mostly Greek and Ladino.
That's the two languages we-- we used to speak.
We were speaking Greek, actually, with our friends.
We had-- we had quite a few Gentile friends, you know, but mostly were Jewish.
Our friends were mostly Jewish, because at one point, when I was in the Italian school, I used to belong also to the-- to Jewish organizations, you know, the-- the Jabotinsky actually.
We were always-- at that time, you know, Israel, you know, was Palestine.
And we were always talking about-- we had somebody there that-- to talk us about-- about Jabotinsky, the big capo of the Jewish people.
No.
I really don't remember too much.
I know that the synagogue was across from-- from a-- a river, you know.
When it was-- we had a lot of the rain, that became a river.
And across from that was a synagogue.
It was very close to-- to the house, in walking distance.
I don't-- I don't remember what it looks like right now.
I attended from-- from elementary school.
Was about the five and four-- nine years.

After I finished school, once I couldn't go to any university-- and my father arranged that I work for the same newspaper he was doing, at that time just translating from Italian to Greek or French to Greek at that time, for a while.
That was in 1938, '38 to '40.
In-- I was 16, 17.
Oh, yeah.
In 1933, was quite a bit of anti-Semitism, especially in the Jewish neighborhoods.
There were two, three neighborhoods.
And then, at that time, was the-- they call it the Three Es were an organization that was against-- against the Jews.
I don't know.
Three Epsilon they call it in Greek was an organization that was against the Jews at that time.
Well, they would try to burn the neighborhoods or do-- you know, put fires.
That's-- that's why my uncle that lived in a different neighborhood, you know, decide to take his family and go-- at that time, go into Palestine.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Sure.
We were all aware what was happening.
But I-- I think since we were Italian citizens, they wouldn't-- they were-- they knew where we were, so we were pretty safe.
We had all kinds.
You know, first of all, we had a lot of Italian-- Italian books and newspapers, and especially Greek newspapers.
My father was working, bringing home all the time the newspaper he was working on.
Yes, always.
I always-- I like-- I don't read books enough.
But I read a lot of magazines and newspapers.
I always want-- want to find out what the world is all about.
Oh, yeah.
We were-- we were reading-- no, especially in 1939 when the war started, you know, with Germany, you know, declaring war to Poland.
At that time, you know, then the Italians also, in-- a year later in 1940, declared war to Greece.
So what happened, during that time, that the Greeks took our family.
They left my father and my little brother home.
And me, my brother, and my father, they took us in a camp, a concentration camp in southern Greece.
In Argos, Greece.
That-- that was the camp.
But since all the Italians-- some of the Italians were very well known and very good friends of the king of Greece.
After a few months, they decided that they will pay their own way and bring them to close to Athens, in Marousi in a small hotel where they were-- the-- the rich people will pay for everything.
And we stay there until the end of the war, for another six months.
We were just-- just closed in a-- in a hotel, you know.
Yeah.
Well, because we were Italian citizens.
And they thought once Italy declare war, we will be with Italy instead of with Greece.
We were Italian citizens.
[INAUDIBLE] you know, we were Italians.
In Argos, it wasn't bad.
You know, it was-- after I went to-- to Poland, you know, that was pretty good, and especially when we moved us to a hotel.
We just stayed there until the end doing nothing really.
Until 1941, for a year.
Both, yeah.
One year.
And so after you left this camp and you went back to Athens--
I went-- no.
We went-- I went back to Salonika in 1941.
At that time, the Germans game in.
In Marousi, which is close to Athens.
And from there, when we finish, we went back to our homes in-- in Salonika.
Few months, five, six months, probably.
Nothing.
We were-- nothing to do, really.
We were just reading and going-- was-- they had a big outside of the hotel, so we stayed around there.
It wasn't bad.
It was very good at that time.
No, not really.
Yeah.
There was a guard, but just on the one garden.
They were very close to the king, most of the Italian Jews, to the king of Greece.
And they had very good relationships, so I think he arranged it to be-- once they were-- it cost nothing to the Greek government.
So after returning to Salonika, what were things like when you came back?
When I came back, the newspaper didn't exist anymore.
The Germans came in.
They closed the newspaper.
And I-- I was trying to-- to find a job, actually for over-- over a year.
Another Danish cousin, the father had a mill to grind up-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the-- what they call it?
To make bread, you know, just the-- the wheat.
They were coming from the-- around-- around Salonika, the people they had wheat, you know.
They had to mill it.
So they came down to us and I worked for a year, you know, all night long actually, doing that kind of work.
Oh, that was from 1941 to 1943, when the Germans started surrounding the-- the Jewish people.
For-- at one point, was OK, the first year.
But then I think the Germans started asking a lot of questions, going to the Jewish community and find out who they are.
You know, they started to have, you know, I think in 1942 the-- what was the-- the head in Germany?
The-- what was the meeting they had for the Final Solution?
Wannsee.
Wannsee.
The Wannsee.
So after that, you know, they-- they start just going after the Jews of Salonika.
There were lots of them.
So this is how-- but we went-- we left in 1943.
The Italian government put us in an Italian, they call it [ITALIAN], in an Italian-- the-- they were coming to Salonika, railroads from Italy, and they took us to the Italian part of Greece.
Half of it was the Germans, and from Larissa down to southern Greece, the Italians occupied then.
And they put us in a school where we stayed there until I was caught by the Germans in the synagogue in March, 1944.
Before we get to that, while you were still in Salonika, before you left, were the Germans that were there treating the Greek Jews differently than the Italian Jews?
Oh, yeah.
Well, we-- they didn't-- they didn't do us-- We had in our door, we had from Italian-- from the Italian consulate a piece of paper that we're Italians.
So the Jews, they didn't touch us.
They didn't do anything, even though that they were complaining to the Italian authorities.
The Italian government said that they shouldn't touch us.
Well, they were- they were doing-- you know, little by little, they were putting in ghettos.
And then they-- I didn't see too much.
I-- I saw when they start taking them, you know, because I had a lot of friends of mine, you know, they went from Salonika.
Friends from school and-- and regular young friends of mine that they were taken.
You know, they started transports.
I was already-- when they start taking them I was already in Athens.
Yeah, went along.
Yeah.
No, but I-- when I was in Athens, you know-- but there were always Jewish traitors around the whole thing.
But you know, they saved themselves for a while, but at the end they all perished.
Was in-- in 1943.
